title: Purpose and Scope of Visual Testing
narrationScript: Visual testing is the oldest and most widely used method in nondestructive testing. On most jobs it is the first method anyone uses, and it is usually the only one performed on one hundred percent of the work. So before we ever talk about lighting or gauges, let's get clear on what visual testing is for, who depends on it, and when it gets done. The scope of visual testing is broad. We are evaluating the condition of a surface and the geometry of a part using the inspector's eye, helped by lighting, mirrors, magnifiers, gauges, and remote tools such as borescopes. If it can be seen, directly or with an optical aid, it falls inside visual testing. As a Level Two, you are expected to know not just how to look, but why the look matters and who relies on it. Visual testing gates the flow of work. A weld that passes can move to the next operation. A casting that fails goes back for repair before money is spent machining it. Because of that, visual testing sits inside a layered quality system. Quality control through visual testing happens at three levels. The first is internal quality control, done by the manufacturer on its own product, catching problems early when they are cheapest to fix. The second is quality control by the customer, where the buyer or a third party confirms the supplier actually met the purchase order. The third is quality control by authorities, where a regulator, a jurisdiction, or its delegate enforces a code for public safety. The same indication can be acceptable to one layer and rejectable to another, because each works to its own governing document. Now, what is visual testing actually looking for? Three broad categories. First, discontinuities. A discontinuity is any interruption in the normal structure of a material, such as a crack, a lap, a pore, an inclusion at the surface, undercut, or corrosion pitting. Remember this clearly. A discontinuity is not automatically a defect. It becomes a defect only when it is evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found rejectable. Second, shape and geometry deviations. Visual testing confirms the part matches its drawing. Weld size and profile, reinforcement height, fillet leg length, fit up gaps, high low mismatch, distortion, and dimensions. A weld can be perfectly sound and still be rejectable because it is the wrong shape or undersized. Third, surface finish. That covers roughness, machining marks, grinding burns, scratches, gouges, spatter, arc strikes, oxidation, and cleanliness. Finish matters because a rough or dirty surface can hide other discontinuities, start fatigue cracks, hold corrosion, or fail to seal. It is also often a precondition for penetrant and magnetic particle testing. Keep the core idea front and center. Visual testing detects and documents. It does not, by itself, decide acceptability. Your job is to find the indication, describe it accurately, and hand a clean record to whoever evaluates it against the code. Finally, timing. Visual testing is applied at two very different points in a product's life. During manufacture, it is woven through production. You inspect base material, joint preparation, and fit up before welding. You check interpass condition and bead profile during welding. You examine the finished joint and part afterward. Catching a problem in process is far cheaper than catching it at final, and far cheaper still than catching it in the field. In service is the other world. Here we examine equipment that is already operating, looking for damage that develops with time and use, such as corrosion, erosion, fatigue cracks, creep, and wear. The part was acceptable when it was built. The question now is whether it is still fit to keep running, judged against in service and fitness for service rules rather than the original construction code. Knowing which world you are in, new construction or in service, tells you which procedure, which criteria, and which records apply before you ever pick up a flashlight.

sections.heading: What VT Covers
sections.body:
Visual testing is the oldest and most widely applied NDT method, and on most jobs it is the first method anyone uses and the only one performed on one hundred percent of the work. Its **scope** is broad: VT evaluates the condition of a surface and the geometry of a part using the inspector's eye, aided by lighting, mirrors, magnifiers, gauges, and remote tools such as borescopes. Anything that can be seen, directly or with an optical aid, is fair game.

As a Level II you are expected to know not just how to look, but **why** the look matters and **who** relies on it. A weld that passes VT can move to the next operation; a casting that fails goes back for repair before money is spent on machining. Because VT gates the flow of work, it sits inside a layered quality system. The same component may be examined several times by different parties with different authority and different acceptance criteria. Understanding that structure keeps you from over- or under-calling, and tells you whose procedure and whose records govern the examination you are performing.

sections.heading: Three Layers of Oversight
sections.body:
Quality control through VT happens at three levels, and you will work under all of them across your career.

- **Internal quality control** is performed by the manufacturer on its own product. Production inspectors verify fit-up, in-process welds, dimensions, and final condition against the shop's procedures before anything ships. This is where most VT hours are spent and where defects are cheapest to catch.
- **Quality control by the customer** is the buyer protecting its interests. A customer's representative, or a third-party inspection agency hired by the customer, witnesses or re-examines work to confirm the supplier actually met the purchase-order requirements. Source inspection at the supplier's plant and receiving inspection at the customer's dock both fall here.
- **Quality control by authorities** is oversight by a regulatory body, jurisdiction, or its delegate, for example an Authorized Inspector working to a pressure-vessel code, a classification-society surveyor, or a civil-aviation authority. Their job is public safety and code compliance, and their sign-off can be a legal prerequisite to stamping, certifying, or placing equipment in service.

The same indication can be acceptable to one layer and rejectable to another, because each works to its own governing document.

sections.caption: The three quality-oversight roles in VT.

sections.heading: What VT Looks For: Discontinuities
sections.body:
VT is hunting three broad categories of condition. The first is **discontinuities** - any interruption in the normal structure of a material, such as a crack, a lap, a pore, an inclusion breaking the surface, undercut at a weld toe, or corrosion pitting. A discontinuity is not automatically a defect. It becomes a defect only when it is evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found rejectable. That distinction, discontinuity versus defect, is one a Level II must never blur.

Surface-breaking discontinuities are VT's strength, because the method reads what reaches the surface. Many originate long before the inspector sees them: inherent flaws from casting and rolling, processing flaws from welding and machining, and service flaws such as fatigue cracking or stress-corrosion cracking that appear only after the part has been in use. You will study that full taxonomy in detail later. For now, fix the idea that VT's job is to **detect** the discontinuity, **describe** it accurately, and hand a clean record to whoever evaluates it against the code.

sections.caption: What visual testing evaluates on a part: surface-breaking discontinuities, shape and geometry deviations, and surface finish.

sections.heading: Shape, Geometry, and Surface Finish
sections.body:
The second category is **shape and geometry deviations**. VT confirms that the part matches its drawing: weld size and profile, reinforcement height, fillet leg length, fit-up gaps, mismatch (high-low) at a joint, distortion, warpage, straightness, and dimensional conformance. Many of these are checked with simple measuring tools - fillet gauges, a bridge-cam gauge, a Hi-Lo gauge - rather than the naked eye alone, but the act is still visual examination. A weld can be perfectly sound metallurgically and still be rejectable because its profile is wrong or it is undersized.

The third category is **surface finish** - the texture and condition of the surface itself. This covers roughness, machining marks, grinding burns, scratches, gouges, weld spatter, arc strikes, slag, oxidation, and cleanliness. Finish matters for function: a rough or contaminated surface can mask other discontinuities, initiate fatigue cracks, hold corrosion, or fail to seal. Sometimes finish is a requirement in its own right; sometimes it is a precondition for another NDT method, since penetrant and magnetic-particle testing both demand a properly prepared surface.

Taken together, these three categories - condition, geometry, and finish - define everything VT is asked to judge.

sections.title: Indication, not verdict
sections.body: VT **detects and documents** - it does not, by itself, decide acceptability. The same crack-like indication may be acceptable under one layer's criteria and rejectable under another's. Your job is to find it, characterize it accurately (type, location, length, depth where measurable), and record it so the responsible party can evaluate it against the governing acceptance criteria.

sections.heading: Timing: Manufacture vs In Service
sections.body:
VT is applied at two fundamentally different points in a product's life, and the mindset for each differs.

**During manufacture**, VT is woven through the production sequence. Before welding you inspect base material, joint preparation, fit-up, and cleanliness; during welding you check interpass condition and bead profile and watch for trouble between passes; after welding and finishing you examine the completed joint and the final part. Catching a problem in-process is dramatically cheaper than catching it at final, and far cheaper than catching it in the field - a root-pass defect found before fill is a quick repair, while the same defect found after the joint is capped, ground, and painted is major rework. In-process VT is frequently a code requirement, and its records become part of the manufacturing data package.

**In service**, VT examines equipment that is already operating, looking for damage that develops with time and use: corrosion and erosion, fatigue cracks at stress risers, creep, wear, coating breakdown, and leakage. The part was acceptable when built; the question now is whether it is still fit to run. Field work brings constraints the shop never has - poor access, dirty or coated surfaces, heat, confined spaces, and the inability to stop production - which is why remote tools like borescopes and crawlers dominate. Acceptance follows in-service and fitness-for-service rules rather than the original construction code, so wall-thinning that would never have shipped can be acceptable on an operating vessel until it reaches a calculated minimum.

Knowing which world you are in tells you which procedure, criteria, and records apply.

sections.caption: Visual testing through the product lifecycle, showing in-process checkpoints during manufacture and recurring in-service examinations.

figures.title: What visual testing evaluates on a part

checkQuestions.question: A surface-breaking pore is found during VT of a weld. Under what condition does it become a 'defect'?
options: As soon as it is detected and recorded
Only when evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found rejectable
Whenever it is visible without magnification
Only if a second NDT method also detects it
checkQuestions.explanation: A discontinuity is any interruption in the material; it becomes a defect only when evaluation against the governing acceptance criteria finds it rejectable.

checkQuestions.question: An Authorized Inspector signing off a pressure vessel to a construction code represents which layer of VT quality oversight?
options: Internal quality control
Quality control by the customer
Quality control by authorities
In-service quality control
checkQuestions.explanation: Oversight by a regulator, jurisdiction, or its delegate - such as an Authorized Inspector enforcing a code - is quality control by authorities, focused on public safety and code compliance.

checkQuestions.question: A completed fillet weld is metallurgically sound but its leg length is below the drawing requirement. VT should:
options: Accept it, because there are no discontinuities
Reject it as a shape/geometry deviation
Ignore size and report only surface finish
Defer entirely to volumetric testing
checkQuestions.explanation: VT evaluates shape and geometry, including weld size and profile; an undersized weld is a rejectable geometry deviation even when the metal is sound.

checkQuestions.question: Why does surface finish matter to VT beyond appearance?
options: A rough or contaminated surface can mask discontinuities; proper finish is needed before PT or MT
Finish has no bearing on other NDT methods
Finish only matters for cosmetic parts
A rougher surface always improves crack detection
checkQuestions.explanation: Poor finish can hide other discontinuities, initiate fatigue or corrosion, and a properly prepared surface is a precondition for penetrant and magnetic-particle testing.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best captures the value of in-process VT during manufacture?
options: It is optional and rarely required by codes
It defers all inspection to the final examination
Catching defects in-process is far cheaper than at final or in the field
It applies only to in-service equipment
checkQuestions.explanation: In-process VT catches defects when repair is cheapest - a root-pass defect found before fill is a quick fix versus major rework after the joint is capped and finished.

references: SNT-TC-1A / NAS410 (personnel qualification framework, general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (visual examination, general practice)
API in-service inspection codes (fitness-for-service context, general practice)
